items.hook: Gridlock isn’t always failure — sometimes it’s design.
items.title: Why “nothing happens” in politics is often the system working
items.lesson: Veto points (committees, courts, federalism) make big swings hard. When headlines scream stalemate, ask which institutions are doing their job — and who benefits from delay. Pair the news with Federalist Nos. 10 and 51.

items.hook: One outlet is a camera angle — not the whole room.
items.title: Read the same story three ways before you decide
items.lesson: Left, center, and right frames change what feels like “the” story. Use AllSides or Ground News as a habit: skim three headlines, then pick one long piece. That’s current events without the feed dopamine trap.

items.hook: Ignore the speech. Follow the incentive.
items.title: How to read a political promise without getting played
items.lesson: Ask who gets paid, who gets punished, and what happens if nothing changes. Intentions are marketing; incentives are the mechanism. Apply this to budgets, appointments, and regulation fights in today’s headlines.

items.hook: The press conference isn’t the policy.
items.title: Passing a law is half the story — watch the machinery
items.lesson: Agencies, funding, and enforcement decide whether a headline is real. After the announcement, look for budgets, guidance memos, and court calendars. That’s where politics becomes life.

items.hook: Winning is coalition math — not converting everyone.
items.title: Map who must say yes before you predict the outcome
items.lesson: Every bill, nomination, and local fight is a stack of must-haves. List the factions and their red lines. Suddenly “surprise” votes look like arithmetic.
